perturbation.original_text: Additional Reporting Person (a): Merck Serono SA Address: Zone Industrielle 1267 Coinsins, Switzerland Additional Reporting Person (b): Merck KGaA Address: Frankfurter Str. 250 64293 Darmstadt, Germany Designated Filer: Ares Trading SA Issuer and CUSIP: Intrexon Corporation (46122T102) Dated: January 7, 2019
perturbation.changed_text: Additional Reporting Person (a): Merck Serono SA Address: Zone Industrielle 1267 Coinsins, Switzerland Additional Reporting Person (b): Merck KGaA Address: Frankfurter Str. 250 64293 Darmstadt, Germany Designated Filer: Ares Trading SA Issuer and CUSIP: Intrexon Corporation (46122T102) Dated: Approximately January 7, 2019
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Dated: January 7, 2019' to 'Dated: Approximately January 7, 2019' introduces ambiguity regarding the precise date of the agreement. This could create uncertainty about the filing deadlines and reporting requirements under securities laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 13d-1 requires the filing of a Schedule 13D within 10 days of acquiring beneficial ownership of more than 5% of a voting class of a company's equity securities. Ambiguity regarding the exact date the agreement was made could lead to uncertainty in calculating the filing deadline, potentially causing a violation if the filing is delayed due to differing interpretations of 'approximately'.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 - Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 - Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.13d-1 Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. (a) Any person who, after acquiring directly or indirectly the beneficial ownership of any equity security of a class which is specified in paragraph (i)(1) of this section , is directly or indirectly the beneficial owner of more than five percent of the class shall, within five business days after the date of the acquisition, file with the Commission, a statement containing the information required by Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) . (b) (1) A person who would otherwise be obligated under paragraph (a) of this section to file a statement on Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) may, in lieu thereof, file with the Commission, a short-form statement on Schedule 13G (§ 240.13d-102), Provided, That: (i) Such person has acquired such securities in the ordinary course of the person's business and not with the purpose nor with the effect of changing or influencing the control of the issuer , nor in connection with or as a participant in any transaction having such purpose or effect, including any transaction subject to § 240.13d-3(b) and (ii) Such person is: (A) A broker or dealer registered under section 15 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78o ); (B) A bank as defined in section 3(a)(6) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (C) An insurance company as defined in section 3(a)(19) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (D) An investment company registered under section 8 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80a-8 ); (E) Any person registered as an investment adviser under Section 203 of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80b-3 ) or under the laws of any state; (F) An employee benefit plan as defined in Section 3(3) of the Employee Retirement Income Security A
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the date of the agreement by using 'Approximately January 7, 2019' instead of 'January 7, 2019'. This ambiguity contradicts the requirement for precise dating when determining filing deadlines, as explained in the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 17 CFR § 240.13d-1, mandates filing within five business days 'after the date of the acquisition'. The term 'approximately' undermines the ability to accurately determine the acquisition date, potentially leading to delayed filings and non-compliance. Therefore, the change creates a contradiction by weakening the enforceability of timely reporting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: ARES TRADING SA ARES TRADING SA By: /s/ Cédric Hyde By: /s/ Luigia Bocola Name: Cédric Hyde Name: Luigia Bocola Title: CFO Title: Finance Manager
perturbation.changed_text: ARES TRADING SA ARES TRADING SA By: /s/ Cédric Hyde By: /s/ Luigia Bocola Name: Cédric Hyde Name: Luigia Bocola Title: CFO Title: Some Manager
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Finance Manager' to 'Some Manager' introduces ambiguity about the signatory's actual role and authority within the company. This can weaken the legal validity of the agreement.
perturbation.law_citation: 15 U.S. Code § 77f - Registration of securities and signing of registration statement
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 6(a) of the Securities Act of 1933 requires that registration statements be signed by, among others, the principal financial officer. The vague title 'Some Manager' may not clearly identify the signatory as a principal financial officer, potentially leading to challenges regarding the validity of the filing and compliance with securities regulations.
perturbation.location: Signing section for ARES TRADING SA

Registration of securities (a) Method of registration Any security may be registered with the Commission under the terms and conditions hereinafter provided, by filing a registration statement in triplicate, at least one of which shall be signed by each issuer, its principal executive officer or officers, its principal financial officer, its comptroller or principal accounting officer, an
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Finance Manager' to 'Some Manager' creates an ambiguity regarding whether the signatory meets the requirements of Section 6(a) of the Securities Act of 1933, which mandates that registration statements be signed by the principal financial officer. The altered title is vague and may not clearly identify the signatory as such, which contradicts the requirement stated in 15 USC 77f (a): '...at least one of which shall be signed by each issuer, its principal executive officer or officers, its principal financial officer, its comptroller or principal accounting officer, an...'. This ambiguity directly undermines the legal requirement for proper financial attestation.

perturbation.original_text: MERCK KGAA, DARMSTADT, GERMANY MERCK KGAA, DARMSTADT, GERMANY By: /s/ Rando Bruns By: /s/ Tim Nielsen Name: Rando Bruns Name: Tim Nielsen Title: Head of Treasury Title: Head of Capital Markets
perturbation.changed_text: MERCK KGAA, DARMSTADT, GERMANY MERCK KGAA, DARMSTADT, GERMANY By: /s/ Rando Bruns By: /s/ Tim Nielsen Name: Rando Bruns Name: Tim Nielsen Title: Treasury Perhaps Title: Head of Capital Markets
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Head of Treasury' to 'Treasury Perhaps' introduces ambiguity about the signatory's actual role and authority within the company. This can weaken the legal validity of the agreement.
perturbation.law_citation: 15 U.S. Code § 77f - Registration of securities and signing of registration statement
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 6(a) of the Securities Act of 1933 requires that registration statements be signed by, among others, the principal financial officer. The vague title 'Treasury Perhaps' may not clearly identify the signatory as having the authority to sign on behalf of the company, potentially leading to challenges regarding the validity of the filing and compliance with securities regulations.

Registration of securities (a) Method of registration Any security may be registered with the Commission under the terms and conditions hereinafter provided, by filing a registration statement in triplicate, at least one of which shall be signed by each issuer, its principal executive officer or officers, its principal financial officer, its comptroller or principal accounting officer, an
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Head of Treasury' to 'Treasury Perhaps' introduces ambiguity that contradicts the Securities Act of 1933. According to 15 USC 77f (a), registration statements must be signed by 'its principal financial officer'. The term 'Treasury Perhaps' does not clearly establish the signatory as the principal financial officer, raising doubts about the validity of the signature and compliance with securities regulations. This directly undermines the legal requirement for clear identification of authorized signatories in financial documents, as the ambiguous title fails to definitively assign responsibility.
